sentences.text: My name is Fela Abramowicz.
First name, F-E-L-A, and the second is A-B-R-A-M-O-W-I-C-Z.
At birth, my name was Rak, R-A-K.
No.
20Th of March, 1923.
I was born in Warsaw, Poland.
All of them, from the beginning?
The parents too?
My father's name was Zalman.
In Polish, he was called Zalman Rak.
My mother was Esther Rak.
From home, her name was Tenenbaum.
And they were born in a small town, both in the same little shtetl outside Warsaw, about 130 kilometers in a place called Losice.
Want to-- you want to spell it?
L-O-S-I-C-E.
I've never visited-- visited that place.
And 3/4, five of the children-- or four of the children were born in that town.
My older sister was Leah, or known as Lola Rak.
That's the family-- later when they married, then they changed their names.
But I'll give them this way in the Rak family.
The second sister was Chaja.
My third sister was Francis, or Frankie, we called her.
And my brother, Israel Isaac.
Then me, Fela, or in the Yiddish, [YIDDISH] or [YIDDISH], something like that, what my parents called me.
And then came a younger sister, Henia and a younger brother, David.
That was seven children altogether.
Orthodox home.
Strictly orthodox, my parents were, but not fanatics.
So they didn't isolate themselves from the outside world.
They knew what was going on.
And the children, well, it was-- after the Russian Revolution, after many upheavals in the countries and all this other stuff.
So everybody became-- got away from the Orthodox, the children, and joined some youth movements, trade unions, you know, more free thinkers.
And this was my older sisters.
As for myself, I was still too young to-- but I got a lot from discussions at home.
And young people would come in and read newspapers, and were interested in politics and what was going on, you know.
Different-- different generation from my parents.
That's what usually happens, you know.
Yes.
I lived in a Jewish area in Warsaw.
The whole neighborhood was only Jewish.
The only people that were non-Jewish were the caretakers.

The ones that had to be non-Jewish, because they had to perform all those functions like turning off lights and lighting a fire on Sabbath, and things like that that Jews couldn't do during the holidays or Sabbath.
And so he had to be a Christian.
Otherwise, in every-- almost in every court was a synagogue.
And everybody spoke Yiddish at home.
And before I started-- I started school, I didn't know Polish.
When I started school, that's when I learned Polish.
Yes.
Everybody lived in apartments.
They were sort of big, huge tenements of the building.
They had four-- four buildings and a court inside.
And most of the childrens played in the court, you know, before they went to school and after school, holidays.
Wasn't a wealthy area, but everybody was reading, and really nice atmosphere.
Very close, friendly.
Being a big town, you had all types, you know.
But mostly, the ones I was associating with was very nice people.
Honest.
Everybody was reading books.
Everybody trying to gain knowledge wherever they could get it.
And it was a good atmosphere.
No, we didn't have relatives, but had visitors.
Because my parents, being from a small town and having a lot of uncles and aunties, very extended families-- well, big families-- everybody had something to do.
One time, another in-law, so to say, stopped over in our place.
So I got to know a few people, relatives from other parts of Poland.
Oh, very-- terrific.
It was a real culture.
Everybody was looking forward to it, especially in Warsaw.
Before Passover, I remember being in school, and I was asked to write an essay on it, the Jewish street, before Passover.
And it was a real picture.

I can still remember how-- everybody taking off their bedding, hanging it up and clapping it, and getting-- real spring cleaning, because it coincides with spring, and the winters are very severe in Poland.
And everything gets locked.
The windows get barred with all sort of things.
Double windows not to let the cold in.
So it was-- when everything gets opened, the fresh air comes in, and things turn from white to green.
And in the same time, there's Passover, you know, this holiday for liberation.
And everybody gets new clothes, and everything changes completely.
Its absolutely indescribable in a big town.
All the activities, and everybody buying here things, and plenty of vendors, and plenty of very big activity.
And children especially.
Everybody gets new shoes and new dresses.
And the next day-- and the seders.
And it's absolutely-- family gatherings.
Everything had to be absolutely perfect.
The Polish family had everything for Passover.
This was something that had to be celebrated.
Yes.
All the holidays I remember.
I loved the holidays.
Being-- we didn't go to school on Jewish holidays.
Being a Jewish area, most of the schools were only Jewish, especially the one I attended.
About 1,200 or so about children, and all the staff, all the teachers, all the administration.
Even the cleaners.
It's all Jewish.
So we didn't go to school on Jewish holidays.
We didn't go to school on Christian holidays.
So we had a bit of advantage in that way.
Girl children-- girls didn't go much to synagogue.
But we went for Purim, for the Megillah.
I loved to go to hear the Megillah.
I went for some [HEBREW], you know.
I loved it.
And that, I think, was most of it.
And I went to the sukkah, of course, when-- when they put out the hats on the thing in the court.
And it was very great.
Children were allowed to go into the sukkah.
So that was great.
The holiday of when you eat outside in a big tent.
You kind of just put up, you know, like on a veranda on the gardens.
So it had to be on the courtyard.
They put out special hats.
You have to pay a little bit towards it.
And the men were eating, and the women were bringing it out from the house, the hat to the men.
And the children had a great time.
We're decorating it with all sort of colorful balloons and things.
It was great.
Very nice time.
I remember it very fondly, my childhood.
And at this time, I wouldn't give up religion for anything, being a child.
I just grew up with religion, and it suited me very well, because I could dream, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That did a lot for me as a child.
What were your dreams as a child?
Well, every child's dreams, you know.
To be better off.
To make my parents happy.
To become somebody.
You know, you read all those romantic books.
To have nice lovers, you know, boyfriends.
All sort of things, you know.
I was always-- wanted to lift myself above the ordinary life, because it was pretty-- I grew up in the Great Depression.
It's pretty tough on the kids that grew up in this particular time.
I just grew up exactly the particular time when that-- the Great Depression started, what, in 1929?
And that's when I was ready to go to school only.
So yeah.
It was all right, till the war.
It was very tough, living in Warsaw, and very, very hard conditions.
Could you explain that further?
Yeah.
There was little money.
And my father, all of a sudden, started earning much less than he did, although he had work, because he was working for himself.
And there was no opportunities.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Great unemployment.
In the severe climate, it was-- you needed, you know, a lot to-- to keep you warm, good, warm clothes to keep you warm when winter came.
Harsh conditions.
And good food to nourish you to get through.
And it was very scarce, everything, you know.
And there's no-- no way of-- very little.
You just couldn't plan a future.
There's almost no future for young people.
They talk about here in Australia, but nothing like that.
My father was-- he had a work room with a partner to people employed.
They would make lots of mass-produced children's shoes.
They used to get delivered the materials and everything, and they put it together.
They went to export, or to ride around the country.
Because most of the-- it was hand-- this was mass produced things.
So he was a maker of.
This was the only trade when he was growing up, being a-- a scholar in Yeshiva.
So he was very learned in Jewish tradition.
A lot of-- he was a scholar.
So the only trade-- it wasn't the [INAUDIBLE].
It was [INAUDIBLE] which everybody took up.
And this was in the Russian tsar.
And he made a lot of money doing this, exporting it to Russia-- into Russia before the revolution.
Stacks of money.
And later on, the money came into nothing when the revolution came, and he had stacks of rubles which, you know, he had to burn.
And he didn't want to take-- being the oldest brother, he didn't want to take over the family business, which was a general store in this little town.
So my younger uncle-- because my father was the oldest son, so it belonged to him, usually, by tradition.
But he didn't want to.
He gave it to his younger brother, and he took his family to Warsaw.
He said he has a better future there.
So after the war-- the first World War-- he took his family to Warsaw.
That's how half the family was born in this shtetl and half the family in-- in Warsaw.
At the beginning, it was very good.
It was prosperous and everything.
But later on, when the depression came, it became very grim, yeah.
And was your mother working?
No, no.
My mother was-- she had enough to do.
In those days, women didn't work.
Not-- not married women with seven children.
Yes.
I started school at seven.
That was the usual start.
And by this time, the Polish government was only-- Poland was only created, or recreated, in 1918, after the second World War.
Because for a hundred years, Poland wasn't on the map, even.
It was partitioned in three parts.
Some to Russia, some to Austria, Austro-Hungarian empire, and some to the Prussians.
And the Poles fought everywhere.
And then after the second World War, they got their country back.
So a lot-- so I had to start from scratch to organize everything in Polish.
My parents spoke very little Polish in the beginning.
Later on they learned.
So started building those schools.
So in this area where I was, there was this Jewish school.
It was called [NON-ENGLISH], because we're going for six days a week in the school, normally.
But Jewish kids in those schools where I went didn't go on Saturday.
And the other ones, the Christians, didn't go on Sunday.
So we went on Sunday to school.
So it was called [NON-ENGLISH].
And everybody wanted to get in.
And they didn't have many schools here to this day.
So it was-- I was pretty lucky to get in.
Some people had to go into Christian schools, but I got into this Jewish school, which was very good.
The teachers were very, very dedicated.
They really brought us up, and it was a second home, you know.
I really-- I really enjoyed my school.
Oh, we're getting a pretty good education, considering today's primary school.
This was seven years.
It started from seven to 14.
They went by-- not by the date of your birth, but the year of your birth.
So some kids were already eight, almost.
I was-- I think I was about seven and a half, actually, because I was born in March, and the school year started in September.
So we were ready to really-- to get into some work.
By the time I went to school, I knew everything, because-- from my oldest brothers and sisters.
So I knew how to read, and how to write, and how to add up, and how to subtract.
I already knew everything.
So there's nothing they could teach me at this stage.
Because being in a big family, and I just always watched them, and I just knew it.
So it was easy for me.
And I selected the classes, of course.
The A grades and the B grades, and so on.
So I always was in the A grade.
And I had one teacher for seven years, the class teacher.
And she was really not just a teacher who came and went, but she really brought us up.
She was even called [POLISH].
It means somebody who brings you up.
Can you spell that?
It's Polish.
It's Polish.
You wouldn't know.
[POLISH].
It's not a nanny, it's not a governess, but somebody who brings you up, not in physical way, but builds your character.
And she built our characters.
She told us that no matter what anybody does, it's useful.
As long as it's useful to society, it's just equally as good as-- like a street-- street cleaner is just as important in-- in society as a doctor or something.
She-- she gave us very good values, and I still use those values.
No snobbery.
Admit your guilt, when you did something wrong.
Admit it.
She taught us that.
And all sort of great values, which I still use this day, and I instill it in my children.
I did instill it in my children's characters, I hope, cause I-- cause I had the good values, which I think they do.
Actually, it was-- some schools later were built-- which were built were bricks and very luxurious.
This one was a wooden building.
A wooden board building, a wooden building.
Very large.
The big, big, huge corridor on both sides.
Nice, roomy, big window classrooms.
And desks, usually two in a desk.
You just sit.
And my teacher-- our teacher even made us in the middle of the lesson go out of the desk and make some exercises so we wouldn't have anything bent, you know, bad backs.
Stretch so we had good posture.
So she made us even every half hour go out of the-- and make some exercises.
And they cared about us so much.
No, I didn't change schools.
Because when I came out of school, I was, what, 14 and a half.
And I was-- I don't want to brag, but I was top-- top [INAUDIBLE] top of my classes [INAUDIBLE] in my schooling.
And I was very wrapped up.
And I just-- so thirsty, really, absolutely literally thirsting for knowledge.
And my teacher called me, and she said, I'll put you in for a scholarship.
And the education was private.
We couldn't get into secondary education.
It was more than-- lighter than secondarily.
I mean, this school was sort of like today, used to be here intermediate, or something like. that.
But I took-- I got my education different-- in a different way.
I went to-- we had very active groups of people which were students or already qualified teachers or doctors, and they took us-- those very intelligent kids.
And so my dad organized us into self-education, and everybody gets their time freely.
And so I studied biology, and psychology, and literature, and history.
And I've read, and I've read, and I've read.
I've read libraries.
I was just very, very keen.
Being in Warsaw, we were exposed to a lot of culture, Yiddish, and Polish, and international, you know.
And I was a music lover, so I really educated myself in this time.
And also I had to earn a bit of money, because I had to contribute to the-- at this stage, I was out of school, so I had to contribute something towards the household upkeeping.
So I went and I helped my sister, who was a very talented dress maker.
And she worked first, till she was already married.
So she-- this sister, Lola, which I'll talk about her later.
And she-- and I helped her a bit, so that gave me a bit of money.
And she let me off, you know, and gave me time.
And this was going on for about a couple of years, till the war.
So when the war came, broke out, we had four weeks of bombing, day and night.
It was a very, very tough, nerve-wracking time.
And a lot of people got killed, and our home was burnt out.
And I had to go-- everybody went from the burnt house to houses of relatives.
So I went to-- I went to my married sister.
The whole family went to live with my married sister.
And then it was tough.
There was no food.
There was no water.
There was no power.
There was no nothing.
Nothing to-- everything was in ruins.
So I thought, we'll have to go and have a look what's going on in the country, where it's-- where we can-- the family can go and live in better conditions.
And then came the news that the other part of Poland was occupied by the Soviet Union.
When was this?
This was-- we didn't know anything before the bombing of Warsaw.
But at this time, Hitler made a pact with Stalin that Poland's going to be divided in half.
So they grabbed one part of Poland, and they grabbed the other one.
But at the time, we didn't know anything about it.
There's no way we could find out.
But when the war-- the bombing stopped, and people started to get around, we found out.
So we decided-- my older sister, Lola, had a husband who came from this place called Novogrudok.
That's the connection.
[LAUGHS] And-- but he lived in Warsaw.
But he went after-- you know, he said we'll go to-- with my sister-- he said we'll go to Novogrudok.
He had his family there, and he had a house, and a-- a small farm.
And so I went there to live for a while.
And I said I'll go and have a look at what's going on, and maybe we can transfer our family and live where they live.
So me and my sister and my brother--
The other sister.
The-- the second oldest, Chaja, and my brother.
We left the other part of the family in Warsaw, and we went.
And we had to walk most of the way, because the rail was all-- all wrecked, you know.
There was no lines.
There was no-- nothing was going, so we walked.
We walked the day, and we rested at night.
Most of-- most of the time, we found some relatives on the way, being a large family with lots of branches.
And we walked 18 kilometers a day.
And we came to the part where it was this river, Bug, which divided the Russian-occupied Poland from the German-occupied Poland.
We had to cross-- cross it illegally, the river.
So we paid.
There was always somebody to pay.
And he had a barge, and he took us over the river, and we came to the Russian-occupied Poland.
And then we went-- went right away-- we had some relatives near Bialystok.
We went to Bialystok.
And my brother and sister stayed there with relatives, and I went after my-- to see what my sister's doing in Novogrudok.
I slept the night, and I said, I'm not staying.
I want to go to my sister's.
And I walked back to the railroad station and went to Novogrudok, and there I met my sister.
And they were overjoyed to hear that we came here.
And my brother-in-law and my brother decided to go back to-- to the German-occupied Poland and get the rest of the family so we would be all together.
So they went there to the border.
And this time, the borders were very strictly-- very guarded on both sides.
There was no way you could cross the border.
